research.title: Partial Prerendering in Production: Static Speed with Dynamic Freshness
research.whyTrending: PPR is stabilizing in Next.js 16 and changes how you think about static vs dynamic; Suspense boundaries now define independent streaming sections.

seo.suggestedTitle: Partial Prerendering in Production: Static Shell, Dynamic Holes

gaps: Most explain the concept but not WHERE to draw Suspense boundaries in a real page
The Uncached data outside Suspense error is the #1 adoption blocker and rarely walked through
No guidance on boundary granularity tradeoffs (too coarse = slow shell, too fine = waterfall)
strengths: Vercel blog is canonical on the vision
Next 16 blog authoritative on stability + removed flags

officialSources.note: PPR stable in 16 via cacheComponents; experimental.ppr and experimental_ppr removed
static shell at edge, dynamic streamed, single HTTP response
cacheComponents:true enables PPR as default App Router behavior

expertInsights: PPR stable in Next.js 16 (Oct 2025) as part of Cache Components; opt in with cacheComponents:true
Build time: static HTML shell + postponedState blob per route; Suspense fallbacks mark holes
Anything in a Suspense boundary OR reading cookies/headers/searchParams becomes a dynamic hole
Request: CDN serves shell instantly; origin resumes dynamic parts from postponed state and streams in
Error: Uncached data accessed outside <Suspense> at build/dev if dynamic work isnt wrapped or marked use cache
Common cause: dynamic API (cookies/headers/connection) called at page/layout level pollutes whole route
App Router only; single HTTP response streams shell + holes
commonProblems: cookies()/headers() at layout level makes the entire route dynamic
Boundaries too coarse -> slow shell; too fine -> request waterfalls
Forgetting use cache on cacheable data trips the outside-Suspense error

strategy.angle: A production architecture guide focused on the one skill PPR actually demands: drawing Suspense boundaries. Explain the build-time shell + postponedState resume model, then teach boundary placement as the real design work — including the Uncached-data-outside-Suspense error as a teaching tool and the coarse-vs-fine granularity tradeoff
strategy.title: Partial Prerendering in Production: Where to Draw the Boundaries
strategy.excerpt: Partial Prerendering is stable in Next.js 16, and the concept — static shell, dynamic holes — is easy. The hard part is deciding where the holes go. This is a production guide to Suspense boundary placement, the errors that teach you the model, and the granularity tradeoff nobody warns you about.
hooks: Contrarian: PPR is not a feature you turn on, it is a boundary-placement discipline you learn
Gotcha: one cookies() call in your layout makes the ENTIRE route dynamic
Practical: the Uncached data outside Suspense error is annoying and also the best teacher youll get
outline: Hook: the whole page went dynamic from one line
What PPR actually produces at build time
The request path: shell now, holes streamed
The real skill: drawing Suspense boundaries
The error that teaches the model
Granularity: too coarse vs too fine
A worked example: a product page
Migration and when to skip PPR
